title: Keftedes
subtitle: Greek meatballs fried until crisp and eaten warm or at room temperature.
excerpt: Keftedes are the pan-fried Greek meatball — made with a mix of lamb and pork mince, bound with bread soaked in milk, flavoured with dried mint, cumin, and a grating of onion, and fried until deeply brown. They are as good at room temperature as hot from the pan, which makes them ideal for a mezze spread or a picnic.
sourceNotes: Standard preparation in Greek home cookery, documented consistently across modern Greek culinary writing. The bread-and-milk binder, dried mint, and cumin combination is the canonical domestic version. Cross-referenced with established proportions across Greek cookery literature.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked keftedes for up to three months, layered between sheets of baking parchment. Defrost at room temperature and serve cold, or reheat in a 180°C oven for eight minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Fry in batches; the pan needs room for the meatballs to brown rather than steam.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape the keftedes and refrigerate on a tray for up to 24 hours before frying.

recipeTools.notes: A wide, heavy-based pan — cast iron or stainless steel — gives the best crust.

content.text: Keftedes are the meatball of the Greek mezze table. Pan-fried rather than baked, they develop a proper dark crust in hot olive oil that baked meatballs cannot manage. The mix is loosened with bread soaked in milk (this keeps the centre soft even after frying) and seasoned with dried mint and cumin, which give them the distinctive flavour that marks them out from Italian or Middle Eastern versions.

A mix of lamb and pork mince gives the best result. Pure lamb is too rich and can be gamey; pure pork can be dry. A half-and-half blend, or even a little beef mince thrown in, produces a meatball with the right fat content and flavour. Chilling the shaped meatballs for thirty minutes before frying firms them up so they hold their round shape in the pan.

Keftedes are as good cold as hot. A plate of them on a mezze table, alongside tzatziki and olives, will disappear at room temperature just as quickly as they would straight from the pan. They travel well, which makes them a good picnic and lunchbox option.

items.prepNote: crusts removed, soaked in milk for five minutes, squeezed dry

content.text: Combine {{lamb-mince}} of lamb mince, {{pork-mince}} of pork mince, the squeezed bread, grated onion, {{eggs}} egg, dried mint, and cumin in a large bowl with a generous pinch of salt and several grinds of black pepper. Mix with your hands until everything is evenly combined — two minutes of kneading produces a mix that holds together. Shape into walnut-sized balls, roughly 3 cm across. Spread on a tray, cover, and refrigerate for thirty minutes.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, heavy frying pan over medium-high heat until shimmering. Working in batches of eight to ten meatballs, add them to the pan without crowding. Fry for eight to ten minutes, turning every two minutes, until deeply browned all over and cooked through. Transfer to a plate lined with kitchen paper. Add a little more oil between batches if needed.
Serve hot with tzatziki and lemon wedges, or let cool to room temperature for a mezze spread. Flatbread alongside is not wrong.

items.symptom: The meatballs break apart in the pan
items.cause: Too little binder, or the mix was not chilled before frying.
items.fix: Add another slice of soaked bread to the mix. Always chill shaped keftedes for at least thirty minutes before frying.

items.symptom: The crust is pale rather than dark brown
items.cause: Too many meatballs in the pan at once, lowering the temperature of the oil.
items.fix: Fry in smaller batches — eight meatballs maximum in a 30 cm pan. Let the oil return to temperature between batches.

items.cause: The oil temperature was not high enough, so the meatballs absorbed oil instead of frying quickly.
items.fix: Make sure the oil is hot enough before the meatballs go in. A cube of bread should sizzle immediately on contact.

content.text: Keftedes are the meatball of the Greek table in the same way that polpette are the Italian table: present at every gathering from a casual family meal to a summer taverna lunch. The word shares its root with the Turkish köfte and the Levantine kafta, all pointing back to the Persian kufta (pounded meat), which travelled across the Middle East and Mediterranean with the Ottoman empire. The Greek version is distinctive in its use of dried mint and cumin, and in being always fried in olive oil rather than baked or grilled.
